sentences.text: OK, Bergen-Belsen was the death trap, complete.
We didn't work there.
We vegetated in the bunks.
We stood in the mud for hours and hours, and we listened to the air raids, which were going above us.
It was hidden in the woods so well that I was told after the war that the British, who liberated us on the 15th of April 1945, have missed it.
That's what I was told.
But anyhow, they found us eventually on the 15th.
People were dying next to you, above you, on top of you about every two minutes.
The thing what we had to do was take the dead bodies and put them on the pile, which there were piles like in a, in a, in a-- when you go in the woods and they've got, they've got cut wood, you know?
Only this was disorganized.
They were just slung across whichever way naked.
It was terrible.
And the stink was something awful.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So that's what we did.
And we stood on the, on the appells for hours.
My mother became very ill, and we knew that.
I, I got typhoid, and I got blind for many days.
I couldn't see.
But being the three of us, we kind of supported each other.
In the sense of the appell you had to stand at attention, and we made sure that we supported so that we looked like it.
Well, my sister was very ill, very, very ill.
But Mother became a huge problem.
And we took her out-- well, you had to.
She had to go to the appell.
And for a few days, we were able to support her so that she looked like she was standing OK.
But she-- we knew that we cannot keep it up with our no strengths and her not being able to stand.
And of course, we got caught.
And she was-- we were ordered to take her to the infirmary-- whatever, I mean, this is a fancy word.
It wasn't even that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My sister was too weak, so I kind of dragged her down the path into the place.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took her, and it was awful.
The place was smelly, as I said so many times, and I'm repeating myself.
But that infirmary-- oh, my god.
It was just awful.
And there were sick people there, dead people there, and on top of each other, propped against the wall.
And it, it was just hideous.
And my mother turned to me, and of course, we spoke Czech, and I will not speak Czech, but I will translate it to you.
She said to me-- my name is Vera, but she used to use the endearment of my name-- [CZECH], how can you leave me in a place like this?
Well, if your heart isn't torn out of you at that point, it was.
And I did not have a choice at all-- none.
I had to leave her there, and that is the last time I saw my mother alive.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I live with those words ringing in my ears.
I know, I know so well that I couldn't have done anything.
But I'm living with them.
Believe me.
We inquired [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about her, and we were not allowed there, and we never heard anything.
And that was pretty much the story of my mother's life.
She was a wonderful mother.
She was a wonderful person.
She was fair.
And she died, which I learned on the-- after the British came approximately on the 8th of April, which was a week before we were liberated by the British.
I am quite sure that, even if she would have made it that week, there wasn't enough medical attention available to get her well.
But one wonders, doesn't one?
I do.
Now Mother was gone, and there was just the two of us.
And we clung together for dear life, of course.
My sister was very ill.
I was, I was ill.
After the typhoid, I somehow [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] managed, not to recoup, but at least something.
I don't know.
I don't know how to describe it.
Anyhow, [INAUDIBLE] 15th of-- we didn't know what the date was, what the time was, nothing.
Here comes an army, and you are scared of the army to start with because, I mean, the SS and everything.
I mean, to us, any person who was in a uniform was scary-- full stop.

Now the British came, and you know, and I've heard it, actually, on the 50th anniversary of the, of the-- on television that the people didn't even cheer when they saw the British coming because they did not even know or care.
We were-- give us another week, and we were-- we would have been gone.
It was as simple as that.
Anyhow, the British came.
They brought all their rations and everything and started feeding everybody with everything they had.
Of course, that was the wrong stuff.
Everybody was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] got the dysentery they had.
They got much more.
We were in terrible trouble healthwise on top of them trying to do the very best because they were absolutely not prepared for this kind of a disaster to find.
It wasn't that kind of a unit to deal with it.
They [INAUDIBLE] us, meaning the DDT went through it.
To this day, if I have got to put any kind of a cream on, I shudder.
And they took us into the SS compound, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was clean and had bathrooms and had bunks, but clean ones.
And that's where we were transported to in the very immediate future.
All I wanted to say is, the minute I knew that my mother was dead, when I walked around the camp-- and that was after the liberation, and the SS were stripped.
And they had to run with the dead bodies to put them somewhere.
I don't know what they did with them at the time.

But I had to close my eyes because I was just petrified that, just by chance, in these thousands and thousands of dead people, I might recognize my mother, which was unrecognizable, [BACKROUND NOISE] I am sure, by then, she would be.
But that was my biggest fear.
Now we were transported into the, which was very close by, the SS compound.
They have brought in the correct group of people, who were sort of ready to deal with the disastrous [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] health conditions everybody was in.
My sister was taken to a truly a hospital section and was being treated.
She had boils all over her body, and she couldn't walk.
And it was just awful.
So that's the end of the war.
Well, the compound has been-- has stayed in existence for quite a while.
Me and my sister have not stayed there for any length of time.
Now, there is a story-- there are actually two stories.
One story was there was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] American Red Cross group came in-- the military.
And there was a soldier.
He was a captain or something.
He had some stripes-- lots of stripes.
Maybe he was more-- forgive me if I didn't recognize the rank.

And we-- my sister was already in the, in the-- being looked after, and now I had to give the names and-- because that was the, the-- now they were taking the names so that they could reunite families or tell the people if we are alive.
This was this summation of the names.
And so he was from Chicago.
I do not know his name.
He looked old to me, but I'm sure he wasn't.
And I told him the story.
I gave him the name.
I told him about Mother.
I told him about Father.
And I told him my age.
And, and he said to me, look.
He said, you are two young children, and I would offer you-- tonight we have got a transport plane going to New York, and I would like you and your sister to be on it.
You have foster parents who will be glad to take you in New York or anywhere in the States, and your sister will get all the care she can possibly get in, in the United States.
And I thanked him very much.
And I said, no, we've got to get back to Prague because my father said that when the end of the war comes, we will meet at the apartment.
And I truly did believe that he will show, but he didn't.
And I didn't take the man on the offer.
So [SIGHS] meanwhile, all the bulletins were going out-- who is where, and what-- who is alive.
And a friend of my father in Prague realized that myself and my sister are alive in Bergen-Belsen.
And he and some other people have got together and got an old truck to Bergen-Belsen.

And about 40 of us from the vicinity of the Czech land were transported through the bombed-out Germany, sleeping underneath the truck, going through the zones of the German and the American and the British.
And you know, all the papers you wouldn't believe-- the, the, the, the red tape which we went through-- but we made it to Prague.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were in Prague.
They still had the barricades in Prague because that was their big hooray at the end of the war.
They were fighting the Germans, right.
It must have been around the 15th or the 20th of, of May.
Yeah.
No, we went through all-- no, not really.
I wasn't paying attention.
It could that be.
I don't think I was paying my-- much attention to any civilian.
But we had two drivers, which were very kind, which spoke Czech and had enough food for us, for the group to eat.
And we slept on the road underneath the truck, and we got to Prague.
We went, as I say, through the zones of--
--things.
And that was as much as I, I, I do remember.
Well, when I got to Prague, this friend of my father's, who must have had some money from my father-- and he kept the apartment for us.
So when we got to Prague, he gave me the money, and there was, there was furniture in our apartment, which was partially ours still because the Germans were in it.
They took it.
And so we went into the apartment, got into the bathtub, scrubbed ourselves, looked like-- I don't know if you know the famous name of a Muselmann.
And it was terrible-- just terrible.
And my sister-- I put her to bed, and I got in touch with the United Restitution.
I think it was United Restitution in Prague or something Jewish something, Congress or-- I don't know what it was.
It was an American-funded fund.
I know that.
And of course, they came, and they got my sister.
And she went right into hospital, where she stayed for six months in a very touch-and-go situation.
But she recovered, and she got well, and touch wood.
She's fine.
She got married two years later, and now she's going to be celebrating her 50th anniversary with her husband in Haifa.
And she's got two sons-- healthy sons, very successful-- in Israel and a handful of grandchildren.
And she is a very satisfied Israeli.
Though she wasn't, wasn't a Zionist, her husband was, and therefore, that's how her story is finished.
Funny.
They used to say, well, the [NON-ENGLISH], which was our second name, girls, they got back.
That's about all.

I-- even my uncle, which was a [? straight ?] brother-- younger brother-- of my father, but he had a mixed marriage, so he finished only for a few months in, in Theresienstadt-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was so scared that we-- that the world will dump us on him that he was absolutely awful.
Like, I went to see him, and he-- and-- with my sister, that was still when-- before she went to the hospital.
And we-- I said I haven't got money for the tram, and he says, oh, it's good for you to walk.
Well, the way we looked-- how could anybody have said to us it's good for you to walk?
I mean, you know, and he-- now, I see it.
He's dead.
And-- but I mean, he was just petrified being a part of a family that he would be caught responsible for these two girls.
I mean, and that's the close.
That's supposed to be a family.
I, I don't know.
I, I don't know.
Did anyone ask you what you had been through?
Did they want to know about your experience?
Not really.
No, no, not really.
Not really, no.
Well, when I went to my sister in the hospital, I went-- I had to go into the, the, the, the-- to get approval for the apartment.
And in that line-up, I met my husband's father, whom-- we are going into too many details now.

His mother-- my husband's mother had a very private children kindergarten before the war, which was in German, and it was a very high-class thing, which my sister went to and I went to, and my parents knew each other.
And when I was at, at the place where I had to get the approval for the apartment, I met my husband's father.
Eden.
Frank.

And he told me that I will not get the permission and that they had their apartment back, and therefore, I should move in with them into their apartment and let go of the apartment, which I didn't have an approval yet for, which I did.
And I moved in into their place, which was just a block away.
Well, they knew my parents before the war, not very socially, but enough.
And of course, they took me in, and they took, later on, my sister in.
And that's the way I met my husband because he was in England and came back with the Czech Air Force in August.
And that's where I met him.
I was 15 years old then.
And that's where we stayed.
My sister then met her future husband through a woman she-- we've met in Bergen-Belsen.
That was her brother, who was in camps and was liberated by the, by the Russians.
And he was in a good enough shape to join the Russian army and liberated, with the Russian army, Prague.
And my sister met him, and she married him in 1940-- whatever.
Three years.
Now, what did I do?
I went for a school year to a, to a school for a catch-up studies, which was useless because you can't catch up after such a tremendous turmoil in your life.
But I took that year, didn't learn anything.
And then I was faced with the thing-- what am I going to do as a profession?
And they've just opened a school for dental assistants in Prague.
It was a brand new idea, and I thought, well, everybody will want to have their teeth.
I might just as well try that.
And I took it, and that was a very good move because, since then-- of course, what I learned there was minimum.
But since then, I have never been pretty much without a job in any-- anywhere in the world.
I always could get a job regardless of language because, if I knew what I was doing, I didn't need a language.
I left in the 1948 just before Christmas.
I left legally from Prague.
That's when the communists took over.
I left with my husband.
He was not my husband then, but we were going together.
I was 8-- just 18.
And we left.
He was a volunteer [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to Israel, and so, well, I went to-- I went with a transport to Israel because that was the only way you could get out at the time.
I was supposed to go to Australia before that, but then I met my husband.
And when the Australia question came in, I decided I will go to Israel because he was more important to me to go to Israel with him.
So I did.
Well, there was a Czech squadron, and he was a pilot, a trained pilot from Britain.
And so he volunteered and, and went into the-- into Israel under the umbrella of a pilot.
And I went under the umbrella of I'm going to Israel to see because I even took a leave of absence for a year from the job.
I was working at the dental clinic, and I took a leave of absence only.
This was in '48.
In December.
Well, they, they were in-- they had the first war that they were-- you know, Israel was declared a state, and, and they were bringing in the, the refugees from all over the world.
And the, the Arabs were stopping them everywhere.
So we, we went on a, on a passport-- on a Czech-- not like an individual passport, but on a list of Czech emigrants to-- through the Austrian and German border.
Then we went into Italy, Naples.
Naples-- they put us onto a barge, like, under, under the, the hulk or bulk or whatever.
I'm not a sailor.
I don't know the name.
Yeah.
And, and we-- then from Naples, we sailed into Haifa, and they had to dodge the Arabs or whoever was there to disembark us.
They disembark us and shipped us right into, into positions into military camps.
I was sent to-- now you got me.
I finished in Ekron, which was a, which was a, a base for, like, aircraft, like, [SIGHS] pilot base.
But before that, I was in a, in a hospital base, and I forget the name now.
I really do-- can't remember.
And that was just a hospital-- huge, huge hospital-- barracks, barracks, barracks, barracks full of people injured and not so injured and so forth.
Because you see, with the dental course, I had a, a nurse's course, so I really did pretty good considering on the minimum of, of education I had.
I came out with a practical knowledge-- not education, but knowledge.
And on that, I built as years went on.
But when I came to Israel, I could be an-- I became a sergeant in the army right away because I already had that knowledge.
And they were very, very short of people, of any kind of knowledge at the time.
So this was a great bonus to them.
As a nurse in, in, in this big hospital.
Then I married my husband in February.
We, we landed in Haifa on the 1st of January, 1949, and on the 17th of February '49, we got married already in, in Ekron.
And then they transferred me from the, from the big hospital, and I was in, in the hospital at, at Ekron, which had its own section for their own people.
And we had some injuries there.
Believe me, there was quite a-- quite injuries, which we had to oversee.
And there were two surgeons.
And I learned an awful lot there.
Look, Israel is full of us-- the survivors.
They are dying off.
Now, people were not ready to speak and talk about this.
You were not ready to listen or talk about it.
Our main aim in life was, the war is over.
Close the door, and let it be.
Start life from scratch.
And that's precisely what I did.
In the long [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] run, it's wrong.
And it was wrong.
You shouldn't close doors on something so terribly shocking and walk away from it, because it will haunt you.
And I'm sure, if the knowledge they have got now-- the psychiatry knowledge and psychology knowledge-- it wouldn't happen.
They would debrief you, as they call it.
But there was not, not a thing like that in existence.
Therefore, what you did-- you picked up, and you went.
And that was precisely what I've done.
And I was fortunate enough to pick-- to have a man next to me who didn't go through it, who was educated, who had the knowledge of-- oh, he was young, and I'm sure, to me, he was-- he knew it all.
But he didn't.
But to me, he did, at the time.
And I was fortunate enough to, to start from there.
And that was a wonderful thing.
Holy Jesus, how many tapes?
